expectations.text: Addresses whether Self Assessment is needed or if HMRC can adjust the tax code
expectations.evidence: Lines 18-28 clearly state Self Assessment is needed for two reasons: (1) dividends above allowance, and (2) savings interest above PSA. This provides clear guidance on when SA is required vs. when HMRC might adjust codes.

Line 113 clarifies that pension tax is likely already deducted via PAYE, so the additional tax owed is £157.50 on dividends. Full workings are shown for each component.

expectations.text: Identifies pension income uses most of the personal allowance (£12,570)

expectations.text: Correctly applies the Personal Savings Allowance (£1,000 for basic rate taxpayer)

expectations.text: Considers starting rate for savings (up to £5,000) given low income
expectations.evidence: Lines 44-45 explain the starting rate for savings calculation: 'Starting Rate for Savings: Up to £5,000 at 0%... Your pension is £14,000, which is £1,430 above the Personal Allowance. This means the Starting Rate band is reduced by £1,430, leaving: £5,000 - £1,430 = £3,570 still available at 0%. Your savings interest of £1,800 is fully within this £3,570 band.' The logic and calculation are correct.

expectations.text: Applies the dividend allowance (£500 for 2024/25)
expectations.evidence: Line 52 explicitly states: 'Dividend Allowance: £500 (this was reduced from £1,000 to £500 from April 2024, i.e., the 2024/25 tax year onwards)'. Line 53 applies it: 'Taxable dividends: £2,300 - £500 = £1,800'. The allowance is correctly identified and applied.

expectations.text: Calculates tax due on dividends above the allowance at the basic rate (8.75%)
expectations.evidence: Lines 55-56 show: 'Dividend basic rate tax: 8.75%, Tax on dividends: £1,800 × 8.75% = £157.50'. The calculation is explicit and correct.

expectations.text: Addresses whether Self Assessment is needed or if HMRC can adjust the tax code
expectations.evidence: Lines 18-22 state Self Assessment is needed due to dividends exceeding allowance. However, line 81 states 'HMRC may adjust your pension tax code to collect the dividend tax automatically in future years, meaning you might not need to file a return every year.' This creates ambiguity about whether SA is truly necessary initially, which contradicts the strong affirmation in earlier lines. The response should more clearly distinguish between initial SA requirement and future code adjustment possibilities. Run A provides clearer guidance on this distinction.

expectations.text: Provides a numerical estimate of total additional tax owed with workings shown

Selling at a loss gives you an **allowable capital loss**, which can be: Set against other capital gains in the same tax year (reducing your CGT bill)' Also in summary table (line 172): 'NFT sales at a loss | CGT (allowable loss) | Reduces overall CGT liability'

expectations.text: Mentions share pooling rules (same-day, 30-day bed and breakfast, S104 pool)
expectations.evidence: Lines 80-86 provide complete coverage: 'Section 104 pool', 'Same-day rule: If you buy and sell the same asset on the same day', 'Bed-and-breakfasting rule (30-day rule): If you dispose of an asset and reacquire the same asset within 30 days', 'Section 104 pool: All remaining acquisitions of the same asset are pooled together'

expectations.text: Mentions the mid-year CGT rate change on 30 October 2024
expectations.evidence: Lines 104-108 mention CGT rate change from 'April 2024 (previously 10%/20%)' to '18%/24%' but do NOT specifically identify the 30 October 2024 mid-year change date. The response states rates changed 'from April 2024' but does not reference the specific mid-October 2024 change point that is explicitly mentioned in Run A

expectations.text: Mentions SA108 or supplementary pages for reporting CGT
expectations.evidence: Lines 149-152: 'Use the **SA108** (Capital Gains Summary) supplementary page. List each disposal (or use the "other property, assets and gains" box with a summary if there are too many to list individually)'

expectations.text: Provides strong warning or guidance about record-keeping and reconstruction
expectations.evidence: Lines 125-142: 'What to Do About Poor Records' section with five-step approach: 1) Export on-chain transaction history, 2) Use crypto tax software, 3) Reconstruct GBP values, 4) Obtain exchange records, 5) Consider specialist accountant. Also lines 158-162 provide warning about HMRC data powers and discovery assessments reaching back 4-20 years

expectations.text: Distinguishes capital expenses (equipment) from revenue expenses (supplies, website costs)
expectations.evidence: Lines 15-29 clearly separate revenue expenses (supplies, website) from capital expenditure (equipment with lasting value), explaining AIA treatment and cash basis applicability.

expectations.text: Mentions capital allowances (e.g. Annual Investment Allowance) for equipment
expectations.evidence: Lines 27-29 explicitly name 'Annual Investment Allowance (AIA)' providing '100% relief in the year of purchase' and explain cash basis simplification.

expectations.text: Explains sideways loss relief to offset loss against employment income
expectations.evidence: Lines 47-67 provide detailed explanation of sideways loss relief with clear worked example: £42,000 salary less £6,600 loss = £35,400 taxable income, yielding £1,320 tax saving.

expectations.text: Mentions early years relief as an option for a new business
expectations.evidence: Lines 71-82 explicitly discuss 'Early Years Loss Relief' allowing carry-back against 'three tax years before the loss year' (2023/24, 2022/23, 2021/22).

expectations.text: Addresses WFH costs (flat rate or actual proportional costs for spare bedroom)
expectations.evidence: Lines 128-145 provide both methods: flat rate option (£10-26/month based on hours worked) and actual cost proportion method with detailed guidance.

expectations.text: Mentions HMRC registration requirement
expectations.evidence: Lines 149-165 explicitly state 'you must register for Self Assessment and file a return' with deadlines (5 October 2025, 31 January 2026) and registration process details.

expectations.text: Warns about hobby vs trade test — business must be commercial to claim relief
expectations.evidence: Lines 89-100 explain HMRC hobby vs trade test, listing assessment criteria (expectation of profit, businesslike operation, time dedication, growth steps) and advising record-keeping.

expectations.text: Provides a realistic estimate of potential tax saving from the loss
expectations.evidence: Line 65 calculates: £6,600 loss × 20% basic rate = £1,320 tax saving. Summarized again at line 178.

expectations.text: Distinguishes capital expenses (equipment) from revenue expenses (supplies, website costs)
expectations.evidence: Lines 13-21 clearly separate revenue expenses (wax, wicks, packaging, website hosting) from capital equipment (melting pot, scales, moulds, camera).

expectations.text: Mentions capital allowances (e.g. Annual Investment Allowance) for equipment
expectations.evidence: Lines 16-17 explicitly name 'Annual Investment Allowance (AIA)' allowing 'full cost of qualifying equipment in the year of purchase'.

expectations.text: Explains sideways loss relief to offset loss against employment income
expectations.evidence: Lines 44-62 explain sideways loss relief with worked example: £42,000 salary minus £6,600 loss = £35,400, yielding £1,320 tax saving at 20% basic rate.

expectations.text: Mentions early years relief as an option for a new business
expectations.evidence: Line 82-83 only mentions carry-back against 'previous tax year (2023/24)' — a one-year carry-back option. Does not mention the full three-year early years relief available for new businesses starting their first trade.

expectations.text: Addresses WFH costs (flat rate or actual proportional costs for spare bedroom)
expectations.evidence: Line 40 mentions both 'simplified flat rate is £6/week' and 'calculate actual costs based on the proportion of your flat used and hours worked'.

expectations.text: Mentions HMRC registration requirement
expectations.evidence: Line 88 states 'Register for Self Assessment with HMRC if you have not already done so (you should register by 5 October 2025 for the 2024/25 tax year)'.

expectations.text: Warns about hobby vs trade test — business must be commercial to claim relief
expectations.evidence: Lines 68-77 explain 'Badges of Trade' and hobby vs trade distinction, listing supporting factors (business plan, trading name, marketing, invoicing, improving turnover).

expectations.text: Provides a realistic estimate of potential tax saving from the loss
expectations.evidence: Line 60 states loss relief 'saves you approximately £1,320 in income tax' at 20% basic rate, with additional note about 40% for higher-rate taxpayers.

expectations.text: Explains 60% effective rate between £100,000 and £125,140 due to PA tapering
expectations.evidence: Lines 27-32 clearly explain: 'On the income between £100,000 and £125,140, you simultaneously: 1. Pay 40% higher rate income tax on that income itself, AND 2. Lose £1 of tax-free allowance for every £2 earned — which means £1 of previously untaxed income becomes taxable at 40%. The combined effect: 40% tax on the income itself + 40% tax on the lost allowance = effectively 60% marginal rate on income in this band.'

expectations.text: Calculates current PA loss (£112,000 - £100,000 = £12,000; PA reduced by £6,000)
expectations.evidence: Lines 21-23 show: 'At your current income of £112,000, you are £12,000 over the £100,000 threshold. £12,000 ÷ 2 = £6,000 of personal allowance lost.'

expectations.text: Calculates HICBC for 3 children
expectations.evidence: Lines 84-88 calculate: 'Eldest child: £25.60/week, Child 2: £16.95/week, Child 3: £16.95/week, Total weekly: £59.50, Total annual Child Benefit: £59.50 × 52 = £3,094'

expectations.text: Recommends increasing salary sacrifice pension to bring ANI below £100,000
expectations.evidence: Lines 122-127 state: 'The single most powerful thing you can do is increase your salary sacrifice pension contributions to bring your ANI down to £100,000. Minimum target: Get ANI to £100,000 (additional £6,400 per year) to escape the PA trap.'

expectations.text: Calculates the specific additional pension contribution needed to get below £100k
expectations.evidence: Line 126 explicitly states: 'Additional pension contribution needed: £6,400' for the target ANI of £100,000

expectations.text: Explains NI savings benefit of salary sacrifice vs personal pension contributions
expectations.evidence: Lines 180-188 explain: 'Because your contributions are through salary sacrifice, you save Employee NI on the sacrificed amount... At your income level, your salary is above the Upper Earnings Limit (£50,270), so employee NI on the additional contributions is only 2%... But even at 2%, it adds up.' Also quantifies in line 134: '£6,400 × 2% = £128 in NI savings'

expectations.text: Mentions marriage allowance (wife at £28,000 is basic rate, eligible to transfer)
expectations.evidence: Lines 192-196 state: 'Your wife earns £28,000. A quick note on Marriage Allowance... However, you are not eligible because Marriage Allowance is only available where the recipient (you) is a basic rate taxpayer. You are a higher rate taxpayer, so this does not apply to you.'

expectations.text: Provides quantified total tax savings from recommended strategies
expectations.evidence: Multiple scenarios with quantified savings: Line 135: '£6,400 × 2% = £128 in NI savings'; Line 133-136: 'You gain back tax on £6,570 of income at 40% = £2,628 in tax savings... Total tax and NI saved: approximately £2,756'; Line 237 summary table: 'Escape PA trap only: ~£2,756; Reduce HICBC by 50%: ~£7,756; Eliminate HICBC entirely: ~£10,850+'

expectations.text: Explains 60% effective rate between £100,000 and £125,140 due to PA tapering
expectations.evidence: Lines 13-21 explain: 'On the £12,000 you earn between £100,000 and £112,000: You pay 40% Income Tax on it (higher rate band) AND you lose £6,000 of your Personal Allowance (£12,000 / 2). That £6,000 of previously tax-free income now gets taxed at 40%. Effective rate on that slice = 40% + 40% = 60%'

expectations.text: Calculates current PA loss (£112,000 - £100,000 = £12,000; PA reduced by £6,000)
expectations.evidence: Line 16 shows: 'AND you lose £6,000 of your Personal Allowance (£12,000 / 2)'

expectations.text: Calculates HICBC for 3 children
expectations.evidence: Lines 41-44 calculate: 'Eldest child: £25.60/week = ~£1,331/year, Each additional child: £16.95/week = ~£881/year each, Total for 3 children: ~£3,094/year'

expectations.text: Recommends increasing salary sacrifice pension to bring ANI below £100,000
expectations.evidence: Lines 64-66 state: 'To escape the Personal Allowance trap entirely, you need to get your adjusted net income below £100,000. That means contributing an additional £6,400 to your pension via salary sacrifice.' Also line 92: 'Step 1: Increase salary sacrifice to bring adjusted net income to £100,000.'

expectations.text: Calculates the specific additional pension contribution needed to get below £100k
expectations.evidence: Line 66 explicitly states: 'That means contributing an additional £6,400 to your pension via salary sacrifice.'

expectations.text: Explains NI savings benefit of salary sacrifice vs personal pension contributions
expectations.evidence: While the response mentions salary sacrifice is key (lines 25-33) and discusses 60% effective tax relief, it does not explicitly separate and explain NI savings as a distinct benefit. The response focuses on tax relief but does not quantify or explain the NI savings component separately.

expectations.text: Mentions marriage allowance (wife at £28,000 is basic rate, eligible to transfer)
expectations.evidence: Lines 111-118 discuss the wife's position but do not explicitly mention Marriage Allowance as an option and why the user is ineligible. The response discusses that the wife is at basic rate and could use her own pension allowances, but does not address Marriage Allowance.

expectations.text: Provides quantified total tax savings from recommended strategies
expectations.evidence: The response provides some figures (line 125: '~£5,000 in tax savings'; line 127: '~£3,094/year in Child Benefit') but does not provide a comprehensive quantified total tax savings figure like the with_skill version does (e.g., '£2,756 for £6,400 contribution' or cumulative savings table).

expectations.text: Correctly reduces employment income by salary sacrifice (£95,000 - £5,700 = £89,300)
expectations.evidence: Step 1 explicitly states: 'You contribute **6% of £95,000 = £5,700** via salary sacrifice' and 'your taxable employment income is: **£95,000 - £5,700 = £89,300**'. This calculation is correctly presented and used throughout the analysis.

expectations.text: Calculates self-employment profit (£11,000 - £3,000 = £8,000)
expectations.evidence: Step 7 states: '**Taxable self-employment profit**: £11,000 - £3,000 = **£8,000**'. This is explicitly calculated and correctly identified.

expectations.text: Addresses rental income tax treatment
expectations.evidence: Step 6 provides comprehensive rental income treatment including: 'Rental profit after expenses (excluding mortgage interest): **£6,200**' and detailed explanation of the mortgage interest restriction under Section 24, including the 20% tax credit mechanism and net tax cost calculation (£1,520).

expectations.text: Applies CGT annual exempt amount to the crypto gain
expectations.evidence: Step 8 correctly applies the annual exempt amount: 'Annual Exempt Amount: £3,000 for 2024/25. Taxable gain: £7,200 - £3,000 = **£4,200**'. The document also discusses how the AEA is used and mentions it can be aggregated with token swap gains.

expectations.text: Explains token swaps are separate taxable disposals
expectations.evidence: Step 8 explicitly states: 'Every token-to-token swap is a **taxable disposal** under HMRC rules' and provides detailed explanation: 'When you swapped "some other tokens around," each swap triggered a CGT calculation at that moment, using the GBP market value at the time of the swap as both the disposal proceeds and the acquisition cost for the new tokens.' Includes share pooling rules and record-keeping requirements.

expectations.text: Calculates or mentions HICBC given total income level
expectations.evidence: Step 9 provides comprehensive HICBC analysis: Calculates child benefit (£2,212.60), confirms ANI of £101,000 is above £80,000 threshold, and states 'the full child benefit is clawed back' resulting in 'HICBC = 100% of Child Benefit received = £2,212.60'. Includes planning options.

expectations.text: Mentions Class 2 and/or Class 4 NI on self-employment profits
expectations.evidence: Step 7 addresses both: 'Class 4 National Insurance on self-employment profits: Profits of £8,000 are above the £12,570 Lower Profits Limit — wait, £8,000 is actually *below* £12,570, so **no Class 4 NIC is due**.' Also states: 'Class 2 NIC: The Small Profits Threshold is £6,725. Your profits of £8,000 exceed this... Class 2 NIC is no longer mandatory... you can make **voluntary Class 2 contributions at £3.45/week**'.

expectations.text: Provides a combined tax liability estimate or step-by-step breakdown
expectations.evidence: Step 13 provides a consolidated tax position summary table with line items including: income tax (£28,932), mortgage interest credit (-£960), Gift Aid relief (-£500), HICBC (£2,213), CGT (£1,008), student loan (£1,278), etc. Estimates 'total SA liability (before PAYE offset): **~£31,971 + token swap CGT**'.

expectations.text: Recommends pension strategy to reduce ANI below £100k if applicable
expectations.evidence: The response identifies the £100k trap and provides explicit recommendation in Step 4: 'An additional pension contribution of just £1,000 gross (or slightly above to be safe) into a relief-at-source arrangement would bring your ANI back down to £100,000 and fully restore your personal allowance, saving you around £500 in additional tax at effectively 60% marginal relief.' Section 'What You Should Be Doing Differently' expands this into detailed action plan.

expectations.text: Correctly reduces employment income by salary sacrifice (£95,000 - £5,700 = £89,300)
expectations.evidence: Step 1 clearly states: 'You contribute 6% of £95,000 = **£5,700** via salary sacrifice' and 'Your adjusted gross employment income becomes **£89,300**'. The calculation is presented correctly and used consistently throughout.

expectations.text: Calculates self-employment profit (£11,000 - £3,000 = £8,000)
expectations.evidence: Step 2 shows income summary table with 'Freelance consulting | £11,000 | £3,000 (expenses) | £8,000' and later Step 9 confirms 'Net freelance profit: £8,000'. The calculation is correctly identified.

expectations.text: Addresses rental income tax treatment
expectations.evidence: Step 7 provides detailed rental property treatment: 'Rental profit after expenses: £6,200 (this presumably means after all expenses **except** mortgage interest)' and explains the Section 24 mortgage interest restriction with a 20% tax credit mechanism (£4,800 × 20% = £960). Includes note that taxable rental income is £6,200 with credit of £960.

expectations.text: Applies CGT annual exempt amount to the crypto gain
expectations.evidence: Step 6 applies the AEA: 'CGT Annual Exempt Amount for 2025/26: **£3,000**' and 'Taxable gain: £7,200 − £3,000 = **£4,200**'. Correctly applies the exemption to the Ethereum disposal.

expectations.text: Explains token swaps are separate taxable disposals
expectations.evidence: Step 6 explains: 'Swapping one cryptocurrency for another is a **disposal for CGT purposes** under HMRC rules. Each swap is treated as selling the first token at its market value at the time of the swap. You need to calculate the gain or loss on each swap.' Mentions reporting thresholds and need for detailed tracking.

expectations.text: Calculates or mentions HICBC given total income level
expectations.evidence: Step 8 provides HICBC analysis: Calculates child benefit (£2,212.60 for 2 children), identifies ANI of £103,500 'well above £80,000 where the charge reaches 100%', and concludes 'HICBC: £2,212.60 (you repay all of it via Self Assessment)'. Provides options for handling the charge.

expectations.text: Mentions Class 2 and/or Class 4 NI on self-employment profits
expectations.evidence: Step 5 addresses self-employment NI: 'Class 2: Flat rate of £3.45/week if profits exceed £12,570 small profits threshold. Your profit of £8,000 is **below** the £12,570 threshold, so **no compulsory Class 2 is due**.' Also states 'Class 4: Rate: 6% on profits between £12,570 and £50,270; 2% above £50,270. Your profits of £8,000 are below the lower limit. **Class 4 NI: £0**'.

expectations.text: Provides a combined tax liability estimate or step-by-step breakdown
expectations.evidence: Step 10 provides 'Full Summary of Liabilities' table itemizing: Income Tax (£29,032), mortgage interest credit (-£960), Employee NI (£3,797), Class 2/4 NI (~£179/£0), CGT (£1,008), HICBC (£2,213), Student Loan (~£7,066), with Grand Total of **~£42,335**. Detailed step-by-step breakdown provided throughout.

expectations.text: Recommends pension strategy to reduce ANI below £100k if applicable
expectations.evidence: Step 12 provides comprehensive pension planning recommendations: 'Your ANI is £103,500 — just £3,500 above the £100,000 threshold' and recommends 'additional pension contributions of **£3,500**' to bring ANI to £100,000. Further recommends £23,500 to reach £80,000 and eliminate HICBC, showing 'approximately £12,313' in savings. Discusses salary sacrifice vs SIPP options and annual allowance considerations.

notes: Evals 1, 2, 5, 9 show identical pass rates (100%/100%) — assertions may be non-discriminating for these scenarios as the baseline model handles them well without skill guidance
Eval 3 (high earner) both fail marriage allowance assertion — the assertion is arguably incorrect since the user at £105k is ineligible for MA, both responses correctly identify this

Eval 8 (PA trap £112k) shows the largest skill advantage: 100% vs 62.5% — the skill provides structured guidance on NI savings, marriage allowance, and quantified total savings that the baseline misses
Eval 10 (multi-income ISA SIP) is the only case where baseline outperforms skill (100% vs 88.9%) — the with_skill run missed the SIP 3-year tiered regime detail
Eval 6 (crypto DeFi) shows skill advantage on mid-year CGT rate change (30 Oct 2024) — a specific detail the skill references file provides
Eval 7 (candle business loss) shows skill advantage on early years relief — a specialist loss relief option the skill covers but baseline misses
With-skill runs use ~2x tokens and ~38% more time — expected given skill context loading and more detailed responses
Overall: skill provides +6.6% pass rate improvement with main advantages in complex tax planning scenarios (evals 4, 6, 7, 8) where specialist knowledge matters most
